1:
"Now concerning the collection for the saints, as I have given order to the churches of Galatia, even so do ye." The witnesses note the directions for the collection for the poor saints. On "the collection for the saints," the grammatical voices note the occasion. Barnes and Robertson note Paul turns to the "collection" (logia, a gathering of money) for "the saints" — the poor believers at Jerusalem, for whom Paul was raising relief among the Gentile churches (Romans 15:25-26).

On "as I have given order to the churches of Galatia," the witnesses note the precedent. Barnes notes Paul had already directed the Galatian churches in this matter; he bids the Corinthians follow the same order — a uniform apostolic arrangement.

The eastern Chrysostom and puritan Henry note the charity: the relief of poor brethren is a sacred Christian duty; Paul organizes it carefully across the churches, that the Gentile believers might minister to the needy Jewish saints. The verse displays the directions for the collection.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul turns to the "collection" (a gathering of money) for "the saints" — the poor believers at Jerusalem, for whom he was raising relief among the Gentile churches (Romans 15:25-26, Barnes, Robertson); having already directed the Galatian churches, he bids the Corinthians follow the same order, a uniform apostolic arrangement (Barnes). Chrysostom and Henry note the relief of poor brethren is a sacred duty, carefully organized across the churches. The verse displays the directions for the collection. The verse marks the object: the poor saints. The verse displays the precedent: the Galatian churches. The verse displays the order: uniform. The verse displays the charity: relief of brethren. The directions display the organized relief of the poor saints. The verse displays the collection: for the saints. The verse displays the pattern: as in Galatia. The verse displays the duty: charity. The directions for the collection for the poor saints display Paul's careful organization of Christian charity: as he ordered the Galatian churches, so the Corinthians are to gather relief for the needy believers at Jerusalem — the Gentile churches ministering to the poor Jewish saints. The verse displays the sacred duty of relief, with the method of weekly giving (v.2).
2:
"Upon the first day of the week let every one of you lay by him in store, as God hath prospered him, that there be no gatherings when I come." The witnesses note the rule of systematic giving on the first day of the week. On "upon the first day of the week," the grammatical voices note the day — and the Lord's Day point. Barnes and Clarke note that the first day of the week (literally "one of the sabbaths," the day after the Sabbath) is the day Christians met for worship; Barnes and Clarke take this as strong proof that the first day was already observed as holy time, the Christian Sabbath, by apostolic authority. The Reformed Calvin is more cautious: the day was chosen because they held sacred assemblies, but (per Galatians 4:10) no day is to be distinguished as a religious service, only for order; Christian liberty stands.

On "lay by him in store, as God hath prospered him," the witnesses note the principle of giving. Barnes and Robertson note each is to "lay by" (set apart, at home or in the common treasury) a portion in store, regularly and proportionately — "as God hath prospered him," giving according to his means and gains.

On "that there be no gatherings when I come," the witnesses note the purpose: that the relief be ready in bulk when Paul arrives, not hastily collected. The verse displays the rule of systematic, proportionate giving on the first day.

The witnesses agree the principle of giving: each is to "lay by in store" a portion regularly and "as God hath prospered him" — proportionately, according to his means — that the relief be ready when Paul comes, not hastily gathered (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott). But the "first day" carries a disputed weight: Barnes and Clarke take it as strong proof of the Christian Sabbath / Lord's Day observed by apostolic authority (the day of Christian worship); Calvin, more cautious, grants a day of sacred assembly but warns against making any day a religious distinction (Galatians 4:10), upholding Christian liberty. The verse displays systematic giving on the first day. The verse marks the day: the first of the week. The verse displays the debate: the Christian Sabbath or only the assembly day. The verse displays the principle: proportionate giving. The verse displays the purpose: ready when Paul comes. The rule displays regular, proportionate giving on the Lord's Day. The verse displays the laying by: in store. The verse displays the proportion: as prospered. The verse displays the readiness: no late gathering. The rule of systematic giving on the first day of the week displays both the method of Christian charity and a window on early worship: each is to set apart, regularly and in proportion to his prosperity, a portion for the poor — on the first day, read by Barnes and Clarke as the established Christian Sabbath and by Calvin more guardedly as the day of assembly, lest liberty be lost. The verse displays the wise ordering of giving, and arranges for its delivery (vv.3-4).
3:
"And when I come, whomsoever ye shall approve by your letters, them will I send to bring your liberality unto Jerusalem." The brief verse notes the arrangement for delivering the gift by approved messengers. The grammatical voices note the method. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's careful provision: when he comes, those whom the Corinthians themselves "approve" (choose, accredit) "by your letters" (with letters of commendation) he will send to carry their "liberality" (their bountiful gift) to Jerusalem.

On "whomsoever ye shall approve," the witnesses note the integrity. Barnes notes Paul leaves the choice of messengers to the church, that all may be done with transparency and confidence, avoiding any suspicion in the handling of the money.

The Reformed Calvin notes the prudence: in matters of money, Paul is scrupulously careful to remove all ground for suspicion, entrusting the gift to men chosen and accredited by the donors. The verse displays the arrangement for delivering the gift by approved messengers.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's careful provision — when he comes, those whom the Corinthians themselves "approve" (choose, accredit) "by your letters" he will send to carry their "liberality" (bountiful gift) to Jerusalem (Barnes, Robertson); leaving the choice to the church, that all may be transparent and free from suspicion (Barnes). Calvin notes Paul's scrupulous care in money matters, entrusting the gift to accredited men. The verse displays the delivery arrangement. The verse marks the messengers: whom ye approve. The verse displays the credentials: by your letters. The verse displays the gift: your liberality. The verse displays the integrity: transparent handling. The arrangement displays the careful, transparent delivery of the gift. The verse displays the choosing: by the church. The verse displays the carrying: to Jerusalem. The verse displays the care: against suspicion. The arrangement for delivering the gift by approved messengers displays Paul's scrupulous integrity in handling the church's money: he will send to Jerusalem those whom the Corinthians themselves accredit by letters, that the bountiful gift be conveyed transparently and above suspicion. The verse displays the careful stewardship of the collection, with provision for Paul's own going (v.4).
4:
"And if it be meet that I go also, they shall go with me." The brief verse notes Paul's readiness to accompany the gift himself. The grammatical voices note the contingency. Barnes and Robertson note Paul adds that "if it be meet" (suitable, fitting) — if the size of the gift or the circumstances warrant — that he himself should go too, then the chosen messengers shall go with him.

On the readiness, the witnesses note Paul's flexibility. Barnes notes Paul leaves it open whether he will personally accompany the collection, willing to do so if it seems best, but not insisting; the arrangement adapts to circumstances.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the care: Paul's whole concern is the safe and honourable delivery of the gift, ready to lend his own presence if that would serve the cause. The verse displays Paul's readiness to accompany the gift himself.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul adds that "if it be meet" (suitable, fitting) — if the gift or circumstances warrant — that he himself should go too, then the chosen messengers shall go with him (Barnes, Robertson); leaving open whether he will personally accompany the collection, willing but not insisting, the arrangement adapting to circumstances (Barnes). Wesley notes Paul's concern is the safe and honourable delivery, ready to lend his own presence. The verse displays Paul's readiness to go. The verse marks the contingency: if it be meet. The verse displays the offer: they shall go with me. The verse displays the flexibility: adapting to circumstances. The verse displays the care: honourable delivery. The readiness displays Paul's willingness to accompany the gift. The verse displays the fitness: if warranted. The verse displays the company: the messengers. The verse displays the concern: safe delivery. Paul's readiness to accompany the gift himself displays his care for its honourable delivery: if circumstances warrant, he will personally go to Jerusalem with the accredited messengers — willing to lend his own presence if that would best serve the conveyance of the church's liberality. The verse displays the apostle's flexible devotion to the matter, and turns to his travel plans (vv.5-9).
5:
"Now I will come to you, when I shall pass through Macedonia: for I do pass through Macedonia." The brief verse notes Paul's plan to come to Corinth through Macedonia. The grammatical voices note the itinerary. Barnes and Robertson note Paul outlines his travel: he will come to Corinth after passing through Macedonia (the northern Greek province) — for he intends to make that journey, visiting the Macedonian churches on the way.

On "I do pass through Macedonia," the witnesses note the settled route. Barnes notes Paul states his fixed intention to go by way of Macedonia, a deliberate plan, though (as 2 Corinthians shows) his movements were later somewhat changed by circumstances.

The puritan Henry and Reformed Calvin note the pastoral care: Paul plans his journeys with a view to visiting and strengthening the churches; his travel is governed by his apostolic concern for them. The verse displays Paul's plan to come through Macedonia.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul outlines his travel — he will come to Corinth after passing through Macedonia, intending to visit the Macedonian churches on the way (Barnes, Robertson); stating his fixed intention to go by that route, a deliberate plan though later somewhat altered (2 Corinthians, Barnes). Henry and Calvin note Paul plans his journeys to visit and strengthen the churches. The verse displays Paul's travel plan. The verse marks the route: through Macedonia. The verse displays the destination: to Corinth. The verse displays the intention: settled. The verse displays the care: for the churches. The plan displays Paul's itinerary governed by pastoral concern. The verse displays the passing: through Macedonia. The verse displays the coming: to Corinth. The verse displays the purpose: visiting the churches. Paul's plan to come to Corinth through Macedonia displays the pastoral concern that governs his travels: intending to visit the Macedonian churches on the way, he will then come to Corinth — his journeys ordered by his care to strengthen the churches. The verse displays the apostle's itinerary, with his hope of a longer stay (vv.6-7). The very ordering of the route, by way of the Macedonian congregations, shows that the apostle measured his journeys not by his own ease or convenience but by the spiritual good of the churches he had planted, gathering up the scattered flocks as a shepherd makes his rounds.
6:
"And it may be that I will abide, yea, and winter with you, that ye may bring me on my journey whithersoever I go." The brief verse notes Paul's hope to stay the winter at Corinth. The grammatical voices note the intention. Barnes and Robertson note Paul expresses his hope: he may "abide" (stay), even "winter" with them — spend the winter season at Corinth — desiring a substantial visit, not a passing one.

On "that ye may bring me on my journey," the witnesses note the expected help. Barnes notes Paul anticipates that they will "bring (set, escort) him on his journey" — help him forward, equip and accompany him as he continues — a token of fellowship and mutual partnership in the work.

The grammatical-historical note the affection: Paul's wish to winter with them shows his desire for extended fellowship and his confidence in their love; the visit was to be a real one. The verse displays Paul's hope to winter at Corinth.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul hopes to "abide," even "winter" with them — spending the winter at Corinth, desiring a substantial visit (Barnes, Robertson); anticipating that they will "bring him on his journey," helping him forward, a token of fellowship (Barnes). The grammatical-historical note Paul's wish for extended fellowship and confidence in their love. The verse displays Paul's hope to winter. The verse marks the stay: winter with you. The verse displays the help: bring me on my journey. The verse displays the fellowship: mutual partnership. The verse displays the affection: a real visit. The hope displays Paul's desire for extended fellowship at Corinth. The verse displays the abiding: the winter. The verse displays the escort: on his journey. The verse displays the partnership: in the work. Paul's hope to stay the winter at Corinth displays his desire for substantial fellowship: he would abide, even winter, with them, and looks for their help to forward him on his way — a token of the mutual love and partnership between apostle and church. The verse displays the apostle's affectionate plan, qualified by the Lord's will (v.7).
7:
"For I will not see you now by the way; but I trust to tarry a while with you, if the Lord permit." The brief verse notes Paul's wish for a real visit, submitted to the Lord's will. The grammatical voices note the contrast. Barnes and Robertson note Paul does not wish merely to "see them by the way" (in passing, a brief stop) but "to tarry a while" — to make a real, unhurried visit, that the time might profit them.

On "if the Lord permit," the witnesses note the submission. Barnes and JFB note Paul subordinates all his plans to the will of God: "if the Lord permit" — he holds his intentions loosely, ready to defer to God's providence (cf. James 4:15).

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the piety: even the apostle makes his plans conditionally, recognizing that all is governed by the Lord; a model of submitting our purposes to God. The verse displays Paul's wish for a real visit, submitted to the Lord's will.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul does not wish merely to "see them by the way" (a brief stop) but "to tarry a while" — a real, unhurried visit that might profit them (Barnes, Robertson); subordinating all his plans to God's will, "if the Lord permit," holding his intentions loosely (James 4:15, Barnes, JFB). Wesley notes even the apostle plans conditionally, recognizing all is governed by the Lord. The verse displays the wish submitted to God. The verse marks the contrast: not by the way, but tarry. The verse displays the submission: if the Lord permit. The verse displays the piety: plans held loosely. The verse displays the model: purposes under God. The wish displays a real visit submitted to the Lord's will. The verse displays the tarrying: a while. The verse displays the condition: the Lord's permission. The verse displays the submission: to providence. Paul's wish for a real visit, submitted to the Lord's will, displays both his desire for substantial fellowship and his pious dependence on God: he would not merely pass through but tarry a while — yet only "if the Lord permit," holding all his plans subject to the divine providence. The verse displays the apostle's God-dependent planning, and states his present stay at Ephesus (vv.8-9).
8:
"But I will tarry at Ephesus until Pentecost." The brief verse notes Paul's intention to remain at Ephesus until Pentecost. The grammatical voices note the plan. Barnes and Robertson note Paul states his present plan: he will "tarry (remain) at Ephesus until Pentecost" — staying in that city, where he was when writing, until the feast of Pentecost (in late spring), before setting out.

On the timing, the witnesses note the occasion. Barnes notes the reference to Pentecost fixes the time of writing and shows Paul intended a further stay at Ephesus, for a reason he gives in the next verse — an open door for the gospel.

The Reformed Calvin notes the diligence: Paul does not leave a fruitful field hastily; he remains where the work prospers, governing his movements by the opportunities for the gospel. The verse displays Paul's intention to remain at Ephesus until Pentecost.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul states his present plan — he will "tarry at Ephesus until Pentecost," remaining in that city (where he was writing) until the feast in late spring before setting out (Barnes, Robertson); the reference fixing the time of writing and showing he intended a further stay, for the reason given next (Barnes). Calvin notes Paul does not leave a fruitful field hastily, governing his movements by gospel opportunities. The verse displays Paul's stay at Ephesus. The verse marks the plan: tarry at Ephesus. The verse displays the time: until Pentecost. The verse displays the timing: the writing fixed. The verse displays the reason: an open door. The intention displays Paul remaining where the work prospers. The verse displays the tarrying: at Ephesus. The verse displays the term: Pentecost. The verse displays the diligence: not leaving hastily. Paul's intention to remain at Ephesus until Pentecost displays his diligence in a fruitful field: he will stay in the city where he is, not hastening away, governing his movements by the opportunities for the gospel. The verse displays the apostle's settled plan, grounded in the open door before him (v.9). The mention of Pentecost is more than an incidental note of time, for it fixes the writing of the epistle to the closing months of the long Ephesian ministry, and shows the apostle weighing the claims of a fruitful field against his longing to revisit the church at Corinth.
9:
"For a great door and effectual is opened unto me, and there are many adversaries." The witnesses note the open door for the gospel and the many adversaries. On "a great door and effectual is opened," the grammatical voices note the opportunity. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's reason for staying: "a great door and effectual (energes, productive) is opened" — a wide and promising opportunity for spreading the gospel at Ephesus, a field ripe for fruitful labour.

On "many adversaries," the witnesses note the opposition. Barnes notes that, far from deterring him, the "many adversaries" (opponents, enemies of the gospel) are part of why he stays — the very opposition marks the importance of the field and the need for his presence.

The Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the apostolic spirit: an open door and strong opposition together call for steadfast labour, not retreat; Paul stays precisely because the work is great and contested. The verse displays the open door and the many adversaries.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's reason for staying is that "a great door and effectual (productive) is opened" — a wide, promising opportunity for the gospel at Ephesus, a field ripe for fruitful labour (Barnes, Robertson); and far from deterring him, the "many adversaries" are part of why he stays, the opposition marking the field's importance (Barnes). Calvin and Wesley note an open door and strong opposition together call for steadfast labour, not retreat. The verse displays the open door and adversaries. The verse marks the opportunity: a great door. The verse displays the fruitfulness: effectual. The verse displays the opposition: many adversaries. The verse displays the spirit: steadfast labour. The open door displays Paul staying where the work is great and contested. The verse displays the door: opened wide. The verse displays the adversaries: many. The verse displays the resolve: to stay. The open door for the gospel and the many adversaries display the apostolic reason for Paul's stay: a wide and fruitful opportunity has opened at Ephesus, and the very strength of the opposition confirms the field's importance — so he remains to labour, undeterred by the enemies. The verse displays the apostle's steadfastness in a great and contested work, and turns to commend Timothy (vv.10-11).
10:
"Now if Timotheus come, see that he may be with you without fear: for he worketh the work of the Lord, as I also do." The witnesses note the commendation of Timothy to a kind reception. On "if Timotheus come, see that he may be with you without fear," the grammatical voices note the charge. Barnes and Robertson note Paul commends Timothy (his young fellow-worker) to the Corinthians: see that he be "without fear" among them — that he be received kindly, not made anxious or discouraged by their factious or critical spirit.

On "he worketh the work of the Lord, as I also do," the witnesses note the ground. Barnes and Chrysostom note the reason for honouring him: Timothy is engaged in the Lord's work, just as Paul is — a true minister, deserving the same respect, despite his youth.

The eastern Chrysostom and puritan Henry note the tenderness: Paul protects his timid younger colleague, charging the church to treat him with the consideration due to one doing Christ's work. The verse displays the commendation of Timothy to a kind reception.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul commends Timothy to the Corinthians — see that he be "without fear" among them, received kindly, not made anxious by their factious or critical spirit (Barnes, Robertson); the ground being that Timothy is engaged in the Lord's work, as Paul is, a true minister deserving the same respect despite his youth (Barnes, Chrysostom). Chrysostom and Henry note Paul protects his timid younger colleague. The verse displays the commendation of Timothy. The verse marks the charge: that he be without fear. The verse displays the reception: kindly. The verse displays the ground: he works the Lord's work. The verse displays the tenderness: protecting the young. The commendation displays Timothy honoured as a true minister. The verse displays the receiving: without fear. The verse displays the work: of the Lord. The verse displays the respect: due to him. The commendation of Timothy to a kind reception displays Paul's tender care for his younger colleague: he charges the Corinthians to receive Timothy without anxiety or discouragement, since he does the Lord's work as Paul does and deserves the same respect. The verse displays the apostle's protection of Timothy, with a warning against despising him (v.11).
11:
"Let no man therefore despise him: but conduct him forth in peace, that he may come unto me: for I look for him with the brethren." The brief verse notes the charge not to despise Timothy but to send him forward. The grammatical voices note the warning. Barnes and Robertson note Paul charges that "no man despise him" — let none look down on Timothy for his youth or timidity (cf. 1 Timothy 4:12) — but rather "conduct (escort, set) him forth in peace," helping him on his way back to Paul.

On "I look for him with the brethren," the witnesses note Paul's expectation. Barnes notes Paul awaits Timothy's return, with the brethren (his other companions); he is concerned for his safe and honourable journey.

The Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the principle: youth and lowly appearance are no ground for contempt in Christ's servants; the church is to honour and assist faithful ministers regardless of age. The verse displays the charge not to despise Timothy but to send him forward.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul charges that "no man despise him" — let none look down on Timothy for his youth or timidity (1 Timothy 4:12) — but "conduct him forth in peace," helping him on his way back (Barnes, Robertson); Paul awaiting his return with the brethren, concerned for his safe journey (Barnes). Calvin and Wesley note youth is no ground for contempt in Christ's servants; the church is to honour faithful ministers regardless of age. The verse displays the charge concerning Timothy. The verse marks the warning: let none despise him. The verse displays the help: conduct him forth in peace. The verse displays the expectation: I look for him. The verse displays the principle: honour regardless of age. The charge displays Timothy honoured and helped despite his youth. The verse displays the despising: forbidden. The verse displays the escort: in peace. The verse displays the awaiting: by Paul. The charge not to despise Timothy but to send him forward displays Paul's protective concern: none is to look down on his young colleague, but rather to help him forward in peace — for youth and lowliness are no ground for contempt in a faithful servant of Christ. The verse displays the honour due to Christ's ministers, and turns to Apollos (v.12).
12:
"As touching our brother Apollos, I greatly desired him to come unto you with the brethren: but his will was not at all to come at this time; but he will come when he shall have convenient time." The witnesses note Paul's earnest wish for Apollos to visit, and Apollos's own choice. On "I greatly desired him to come unto you," the grammatical voices note Paul's wish. Barnes and Robertson note Paul "greatly desired" (strongly urged) Apollos to go to Corinth with the brethren — showing no jealousy of Apollos (whom some Corinthians had set up as a rival, 1:12), but warmly wishing for his ministry among them.

On "his will was not at all to come at this time," the witnesses note Apollos's decision. Barnes notes that Apollos himself was unwilling to come just then — his own judgment (perhaps to avoid fostering the factions that bore his name, or for other reasons) was against it; Paul respects his free choice.

The Reformed Calvin notes the harmony: Paul and Apollos are shown to be in full accord, Paul urging and Apollos declining for good reason — a rebuke to the Corinthian parties that had set them against each other. The verse displays Paul's wish for Apollos's visit and Apollos's own choice.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul "greatly desired" Apollos to go to Corinth, showing no jealousy of the man some had set up as a rival but warmly wishing for his ministry (Barnes, Robertson); yet Apollos himself was unwilling to come just then, his own judgment against it (perhaps to avoid fostering the factions), which Paul respects (Barnes). Calvin notes Paul and Apollos in full accord, a rebuke to the parties that set them against each other. The verse displays the matter of Apollos. The verse marks the wish: greatly desired him. The verse displays the choice: his will not to come. The verse displays the harmony: no jealousy. The verse displays the rebuke: to the factions. The matter displays Paul and Apollos in full accord. The verse displays the desiring: of Paul. The verse displays the declining: of Apollos. The verse displays the concord: between them. Paul's earnest wish for Apollos to visit, and Apollos's own choice to defer, display the full harmony between the two — a standing rebuke to the Corinthian parties: Paul, far from jealous, urged Apollos to come, and Apollos in his own free judgment declined for the present. The verse displays the concord of the apostles against the factions, and gives the closing exhortations (vv.13-14).
13:
"Watch ye, stand fast in the faith, quit you like men, be strong." The witnesses note the fourfold exhortation to watchfulness, steadfastness, courage, and strength. On "watch ye," the grammatical voices note vigilance. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's stirring series of commands: "watch ye" — be vigilant, alert, on guard against temptation, error, and the enemy.

On "stand fast in the faith," the witnesses note steadfastness. Barnes notes "stand fast in the faith" — be firm, immovable in the truth of the gospel, not carried away by error (a pointed charge given their wavering on the resurrection).

On "quit you like men, be strong," the puritan Henry and Reformed Calvin note the call to courage and strength. Barnes and Henry note "quit you like men" (acquit yourselves as men, be manly, courageous) and "be strong" (be vigorous, mighty) — a martial summons to manly courage and strength in the Christian warfare.

The witnesses note the spirit: these terse commands rouse the Corinthians, given to division and weakness, to vigilant, steadfast, courageous, strong Christian living. The verse displays the fourfold exhortation to watchfulness, steadfastness, courage, and strength.

The witnesses agree this is a stirring series of commands: "watch ye" (be vigilant, on guard against temptation and error); "stand fast in the faith" (be firm, immovable in the gospel, a pointed charge given their wavering); "quit you like men" (acquit yourselves manfully, be courageous); and "be strong" (be vigorous, mighty) — a martial summons to manly Christian living (Barnes, Robertson, Henry). The terse commands rouse the divided, weak Corinthians to vigilant, steadfast, courageous strength. The verse displays the fourfold exhortation. The verse marks the watchfulness: be vigilant. The verse displays the steadfastness: stand fast. The verse displays the courage: quit you like men. The verse displays the strength: be strong. The exhortation displays a martial summons to vigorous Christian living. The verse displays the watching: alert. The verse displays the standing: firm. The verse displays the manliness: courageous. The fourfold exhortation to watchfulness, steadfastness, courage, and strength displays Paul's stirring call to the divided and wavering Corinthians: they are to be vigilant, immovable in the faith, manly in courage, and strong — a martial summons to vigorous, steadfast Christian living. The verse displays the bracing charge to spiritual manhood, tempered by the rule of love (v.14).
14:
"Let all your things be done with charity." The brief verse notes that all is to be done in love. The grammatical voices note the governing rule. Barnes and Calvin note Paul crowns the bracing commands of v.13 with the rule of love: "let all your things be done with charity (love)" — all their conduct, including their courage and steadfastness, is to be governed and tempered by love.

On the balance, the witnesses note the necessity. Barnes notes that manly courage and firmness, without love, would degenerate into harshness and faction (the very fault at Corinth); love is the indispensable atmosphere of all Christian action.

The Reformed Calvin notes the harmony: the strength and steadfastness just enjoined are not to breed contention; love must rule all, binding the whole life together — the summary of the epistle's great theme (ch. 13). The verse displays that all is to be done in love.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul crowns the bracing commands of v.13 with the rule of love — "let all your things be done with charity," all their conduct, including their courage and steadfastness, governed and tempered by love (Barnes, Calvin); for manly firmness without love would degenerate into harshness and faction (the Corinthian fault), love being the indispensable atmosphere of all Christian action (Barnes). Calvin notes the strength enjoined is not to breed contention; love must rule all (ch. 13). The verse displays all done in love. The verse marks the rule: with charity. The verse displays the scope: all your things. The verse displays the balance: courage tempered by love. The verse displays the theme: the epistle's summary. The rule displays love governing all Christian conduct. The verse displays the doing: all things. The verse displays the love: governing. The verse displays the temper: of strength. That all is to be done in love displays the governing rule that crowns the exhortation: the courage, steadfastness, and strength just enjoined are all to be exercised in love — for firmness without love breeds the very harshness and faction that marred Corinth, and love must be the atmosphere of every Christian act. The verse displays the supremacy of love over all conduct, and commends the household of Stephanas (vv.15-18).
15:
"I beseech you, brethren, (ye know the house of Stephanas, that it is the firstfruits of Achaia, and that they have addicted themselves to the ministry of the saints,)" The witnesses note the commendation of the devoted household of Stephanas. On "the house of Stephanas, the firstfruits of Achaia," the grammatical voices note the honour. Barnes and Robertson note Stephanas and his household were "the firstfruits of Achaia" — among the first converts in that region (the Greek province), the earliest fruit of the gospel there, and so worthy of special regard.

On "addicted themselves to the ministry of the saints," the witnesses note their devotion. Barnes and Calvin note they had "addicted themselves" (devoted, given themselves up) to "the ministry (service) of the saints" — voluntarily dedicating themselves to serving and helping fellow believers; a model of self-giving service.

The Reformed Calvin notes the commendation: such devoted servants of the church deserve honour and recognition; Paul holds them up as examples and bespeaks for them the church's regard. The verse displays the commendation of the devoted household of Stephanas.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Stephanas and his household were "the firstfruits of Achaia" — among the first converts in that region, the earliest fruit of the gospel, worthy of special regard (Barnes, Robertson); and had "addicted themselves" (devoted, given themselves up) to "the ministry of the saints," voluntarily dedicating themselves to serving fellow believers, a model of self-giving service (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin notes such devoted servants deserve honour, held up as examples. The verse displays the commendation of Stephanas's house. The verse marks the honour: the firstfruits of Achaia. The verse displays the devotion: addicted to ministry. The verse displays the service: to the saints. The verse displays the example: self-giving. The commendation displays a household devoted to serving the church. The verse displays the firstfruits: early converts. The verse displays the dedication: to ministry. The verse displays the service: of the saints. The commendation of the devoted household of Stephanas displays the honour due to self-giving service: the firstfruits of Achaia, they had voluntarily dedicated themselves to the ministry of the saints — a model of devotion that Paul holds up for the church's regard. The verse displays the worthy example of dedicated service, calling for submission to such (v.16).
16:
"That ye submit yourselves unto such, and to every one that helpeth with us, and laboureth." The brief verse notes the call to submit to devoted labourers in the church. The grammatical voices note the exhortation. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's request (completing v.15): that the Corinthians "submit themselves" (yield, defer, give due regard) to such devoted servants as Stephanas — and to "every one that helpeth with us, and laboureth" — all who share in the work and toil of the gospel.

On "submit yourselves," the witnesses note the proper deference. Barnes notes that those who give themselves to the church's service deserve the respect and willing cooperation of the rest; the church is to honour and follow its faithful labourers.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the principle: voluntary service in the church earns a real (though not lordly) claim to the deference of those served; faithful labourers are to be esteemed and supported. The verse displays the call to submit to devoted labourers.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul requests that the Corinthians "submit themselves" (yield, defer, give due regard) to such devoted servants as Stephanas, and to "every one that helpeth with us, and laboureth" — all who share in the gospel's work (Barnes, Robertson); those who give themselves to the church's service deserving the respect and willing cooperation of the rest (Barnes). Wesley notes voluntary service earns a real claim to deference; faithful labourers are to be esteemed. The verse displays the call to submit to labourers. The verse marks the request: submit to such. The verse displays the scope: all who labour. The verse displays the deference: due regard. The verse displays the principle: honour the faithful. The call displays the church deferring to its devoted servants. The verse displays the submission: to such. The verse displays the helpers: who labour. The verse displays the esteem: their due. The call to submit to devoted labourers in the church displays the honour owed to faithful service: the Corinthians are to defer to such as Stephanas and to all who share the work and toil of the gospel — voluntary service earning the willing regard of those served. The verse displays the deference due to faithful labourers, and rejoices in the recent visitors (vv.17-18).
17:
"I am glad of the coming of Stephanas and Fortunatus and Achaicus: for that which was lacking on your part they have supplied." The brief verse notes Paul's gladness at the visit of the three Corinthian messengers. The grammatical voices note the joy. Barnes and Robertson note Paul rejoices at the coming of Stephanas, Fortunatus, and Achaicus — three Corinthian believers who had come to him (perhaps bearing the church's letter and gifts), whose visit gladdened him.

On "that which was lacking on your part they have supplied," the witnesses note the supply. Barnes notes that their presence "supplied" what was lacking — they made up, by their personal coming, for the absence of the whole church, bringing Paul the fellowship and information (and perhaps gifts) he could not have from the distant Corinthians.

The grammatical-historical note the affection: the visit of these brethren refreshed Paul, who was far from the church he loved; they were, as it were, the church's representatives bringing him comfort. The verse displays Paul's gladness at the visit of the three messengers.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul rejoices at the coming of Stephanas, Fortunatus, and Achaicus — three Corinthian believers who had come to him (perhaps bearing the church's letter and gifts), whose visit gladdened him (Barnes, Robertson); their presence "supplying" what was lacking, making up by their personal coming for the absence of the whole church, bringing fellowship and information (Barnes). The grammatical-historical note the visit refreshed Paul, far from the church he loved. The verse displays Paul's gladness. The verse marks the joy: glad of their coming. The verse displays the three: Stephanas, Fortunatus, Achaicus. The verse displays the supply: what was lacking. The verse displays the affection: representatives of the church. The gladness displays the visit refreshing the absent apostle. The verse displays the coming: of the three. The verse displays the supplying: the church's absence. The verse displays the comfort: brought to Paul. Paul's gladness at the visit of the three Corinthian messengers displays the comfort of Christian fellowship: the coming of Stephanas, Fortunatus, and Achaicus gladdened him, their presence supplying what the distance of the whole church had lacked — bringing the absent apostle the fellowship he longed for. The verse displays the refreshment of the visit, deepened in their effect on his spirit (v.18).
18:
"For they have refreshed my spirit and yours: therefore acknowledge ye them that are such." The brief verse notes that the visitors refreshed Paul's spirit, and the call to acknowledge them. The grammatical voices note the refreshment. Barnes and Robertson note that these brethren "refreshed my spirit and yours" — their coming brought Paul comfort and rest of mind, and (by their report and return) would refresh the Corinthians too; mutual encouragement.

On "acknowledge ye them that are such," the witnesses note the charge. Barnes notes Paul bids the church "acknowledge" (recognize, honour, give due regard to) "such" men — those who thus serve and refresh; they are to be esteemed and welcomed.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the principle: those who refresh the spirits of others in the Lord's work deserve grateful recognition; the church is to value such helpful and devoted members. The verse displays that the visitors refreshed Paul's spirit, and the call to acknowledge them.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: these brethren "refreshed my spirit and yours" — their coming brought Paul comfort and would refresh the Corinthians too, mutual encouragement (Barnes, Robertson); and Paul bids the church "acknowledge" (recognize, honour) "such" men who thus serve, esteeming and welcoming them (Barnes). Wesley notes those who refresh others in the Lord's work deserve grateful recognition. The verse displays the refreshment and the charge. The verse marks the refreshment: my spirit and yours. The verse displays the charge: acknowledge such. The verse displays the recognition: honour them. The verse displays the principle: value devoted members. The refreshment displays the visitors esteemed for their service. The verse displays the spirit: refreshed. The verse displays the acknowledgment: due. The verse displays the value: of such men. That the visitors refreshed Paul's spirit, and the call to acknowledge them, display the grateful recognition owed to devoted servants: the coming of these brethren brought comfort to Paul and would to the church, and such men, who refresh the spirits of others in the Lord's work, are to be honoured and esteemed. The verse displays the honour due to refreshing fellow-workers, and passes to the closing greetings (vv.19-20).
19:
"The churches of Asia salute you. Aquila and Priscilla salute you much in the Lord, with the church that is in their house." The witnesses note the greetings from the Asian churches and from Aquila and Priscilla. On "the churches of Asia salute you," the grammatical voices note the greeting. Barnes and Robertson note Paul conveys greetings from "the churches of Asia" (the Roman province of Asia, around Ephesus where he was writing) — a token of the fellowship binding the churches together.

On "Aquila and Priscilla... with the church in their house," the witnesses note the couple. Barnes and Henry note the warm greeting from Aquila and Priscilla (Paul's tentmaker friends and fellow-workers, Acts 18) "much in the Lord," together with "the church that is in their house" — a Christian congregation meeting in their home, a notable early instance of a house-church.

The puritan Henry and grammatical-historical note the affection: these greetings express the love and communion of the saints across the churches; the house-church of Aquila and Priscilla shows the devotion of this godly couple. The verse displays the greetings from the Asian churches and from Aquila and Priscilla.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul conveys greetings from "the churches of Asia" (the province around Ephesus, where he wrote), a token of fellowship binding the churches (Barnes, Robertson); and the warm greeting from Aquila and Priscilla (his tentmaker friends and fellow-workers, Acts 18) "much in the Lord," with "the church in their house" — a congregation meeting in their home, a notable early house-church (Barnes, Henry). Henry notes the love and communion of the saints, and the devotion of this godly couple. The verse displays the greetings. The verse marks the churches: of Asia. The verse displays the couple: Aquila and Priscilla. The verse displays the house-church: in their home. The verse displays the affection: the communion of saints. The greetings display the fellowship binding the churches in love. The verse displays the salute: from Asia. The verse displays the friends: Aquila and Priscilla. The verse displays the church: in their house. The greetings from the Asian churches and from Aquila and Priscilla display the warm communion of the saints across the churches: Paul conveys the love of the Asian congregations and of his devoted fellow-workers, in whose home a church met — a token of the fellowship and devotion that bound the early believers. The verse displays the affectionate greetings of the churches, joined by all the brethren (v.20).
20:
"All the brethren greet you. Greet ye one another with an holy kiss." The brief verse notes the greeting of all the brethren and the holy kiss. The grammatical voices note the salutation. Barnes and Robertson note Paul adds that "all the brethren" (all the believers with him) greet the Corinthians — the universal love of the saints — and bids them "greet one another with an holy kiss."

On "an holy kiss," the witnesses note the custom. Barnes and Calvin note the "holy kiss" was the customary token of Christian love and fellowship in the early church (Romans 16:16) — a kiss of peace, "holy" (chaste, pure, sacred), expressing brotherly affection and unity among believers.

The eastern Cyril and Reformed Calvin note the meaning: the holy kiss signified reconciliation and mutual love; for the divided Corinthians especially, it was a call to put away strife and embrace one another in Christian affection. The verse displays the greeting of all the brethren and the holy kiss.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: "all the brethren" (the believers with Paul) greet the Corinthians, the universal love of the saints (Barnes, Robertson); and they are to "greet one another with an holy kiss" — the customary token of Christian love and fellowship (Romans 16:16), a kiss of peace, "holy" (chaste, sacred), expressing brotherly affection and unity (Barnes, Calvin). Cyril and Calvin note it signified reconciliation and mutual love, a call to the divided Corinthians to put away strife. The verse displays the greeting and the holy kiss. The verse marks the greeting: all the brethren. The verse displays the custom: an holy kiss. The verse displays the meaning: love and fellowship. The verse displays the call: to reconciliation. The greeting displays the saints' mutual love and the kiss of peace. The verse displays the brethren: all greeting. The verse displays the kiss: holy. The verse displays the affection: brotherly. The greeting of all the brethren and the holy kiss display the mutual love that binds the church: the believers with Paul send their love, and the Corinthians are to greet one another with the holy kiss — the token of Christian fellowship and a call, to that divided church, to embrace one another in pure affection and peace. The verse displays the sign of brotherly love, before Paul's own hand (v.21).
21:
"The salutation of me Paul with mine own hand." The brief verse notes Paul's authenticating greeting in his own hand. The grammatical voices note the practice. Barnes and Robertson note that Paul, who usually dictated his letters to an amanuensis (a scribe), here adds the closing salutation "with mine own hand" — writing it personally as a mark of authenticity and affection.

On the purpose, the witnesses note the authentication. Barnes notes this autograph greeting was Paul's customary token (2 Thessalonians 3:17) by which his letters were known to be genuine, guarding against forgery; it also added a personal warmth.

The grammatical-historical note the significance: the personally written close certified the letter as truly Paul's, and lent his own hand to the affectionate greeting and the solemn words that follow. The verse displays Paul's authenticating greeting in his own hand.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul, who usually dictated to a scribe, here adds the closing salutation "with mine own hand," writing it personally as a mark of authenticity and affection (Barnes, Robertson); this autograph being his customary token (2 Thessalonians 3:17) by which his letters were known genuine, guarding against forgery (Barnes). The grammatical-historical note the personally written close certified the letter as truly Paul's. The verse displays Paul's own-hand greeting. The verse marks the practice: mine own hand. The verse displays the purpose: authentication. The verse displays the token: against forgery. The verse displays the warmth: personal. The greeting displays Paul's autograph certifying the letter. The verse displays the salutation: of Paul. The verse displays the hand: his own. The verse displays the authenticity: certified. Paul's authenticating greeting in his own hand displays his customary mark of genuineness: having dictated the letter to a scribe, he writes the closing salutation himself — certifying it as truly his, guarding against forgery, and lending his own hand to the affectionate and solemn words that close the epistle. The verse displays the apostle's personal seal, followed by the weighty warning (v.22). That the apostle should break off from dictation to take the pen into his own hand at this point lends a peculiar solemnity to the words that immediately follow, as though he would set the whole weight of his personal authority and affection behind both the warning and the blessing with which the letter closes.
22:
"If any man love not the Lord Jesus Christ, let him be Anathema Maranatha." The witnesses note the solemn curse on those who love not Christ — and the Maranatha crux. On "if any man love not the Lord Jesus Christ, let him be Anathema," the grammatical voices note the test and the anathema. Barnes and Calvin note Paul makes love to Christ the decisive test of true Christianity, and pronounces that whoever lacks it is "Anathema" (accursed, devoted to destruction). Most stress this is not a vindictive wish but a declaration of fact — a solemn statement of what is true of, and what ought to befall, the one who does not love Christ; Calvin reads it "let him be held by you as accursed," abhorred by the church; Clarke as a prediction of judgment (especially on the unbelieving Jews).

On "Maranatha," the debate divides. The grammatical-historical Barnes, Robertson, and Ellicott take it as a separate Aramaic phrase — "the Lord cometh" (or "Our Lord, come") — wrongly joined to "Anathema" by the King James Version; it is a watchword of the Lord's coming, added (per Barnes, Clarke, Wesley) as a solemn reminder that the Lord will come to execute judgment on those who love him not. Some render it "O Lord, come" (a prayer); most, "the Lord cometh / is come."

The witnesses note the force: the absence of love to Christ is the one fatal lack, and the Lord's coming will vindicate this solemn sentence. The verse displays the curse on those who love not Christ, with the Maranatha crux.

The witnesses agree Paul makes love to Christ the decisive test, pronouncing the loveless "Anathema" (accursed) — most stressing this is a declaration of fact (or, with Calvin, "held as accursed" by the church; with Clarke, a prediction of judgment), not a vindictive wish. And "Maranatha" they take as a separate Aramaic phrase, "the Lord cometh" (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott, Calvin, Wesley), wrongly joined to "Anathema" by the King James Version — a watchword of the Lord's coming, added as a solemn reminder that he will come to judge those who love him not (some render it "O Lord, come"). The verse displays the curse and the Maranatha crux. The verse marks the test: love to Christ. The verse displays the anathema: accursed. The verse displays the debate: Maranatha's meaning and connection. The verse displays the force: the Lord's coming. The curse displays love to Christ as the one fatal test. The verse displays the love: lacking. The verse displays the anathema: pronounced. The verse displays the Maranatha: the Lord cometh. The solemn curse on those who love not Christ, with the Maranatha crux, displays the decisive test of the epistle's close: whoever lacks love to the Lord Jesus is accursed (a declaration of fact, not a vindictive wish), and "Maranatha" — "the Lord cometh," a separate watchword wrongly joined to the curse by the KJV — warns that his coming will execute the sentence. The verse displays the supreme importance of love to Christ, sealed by the closing benediction (vv.23-24).
23:
"The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with you." The brief verse notes the apostolic benediction of grace. The grammatical voices note the blessing. Barnes and Gill note Paul closes with his customary benediction: "the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with you" — invoking upon them the free favour, love, and saving help of Christ, the sum of every blessing.

On "the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ," the witnesses note the source. Barnes notes that grace is ascribed to Christ as its source and channel — a mark of his divine dignity; the blessing Paul most desires for them is the abiding favour of the Lord Jesus.

The eastern Cyril notes the fitness: after the solemn warning of v.22, Paul turns to bless those who do love Christ, that his grace may rest upon them — the gentle close balancing the severe sentence. The verse displays the apostolic benediction of grace.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul closes with his customary benediction — "the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with you," invoking the free favour, love, and saving help of Christ, the sum of every blessing (Barnes, Gill); grace ascribed to Christ as its source, a mark of his divine dignity, the abiding favour of the Lord Jesus being the blessing Paul most desires for them (Barnes). Cyril notes that after the solemn warning, Paul blesses those who love Christ, the gentle close balancing the severe sentence. The verse displays the benediction of grace. The verse marks the blessing: the grace of Christ. The verse displays the source: Christ. The verse displays the dignity: grace from him. The verse displays the balance: after the warning. The benediction displays the grace of Christ invoked on the church. The verse displays the grace: bestowed. The verse displays the Lord: its source. The verse displays the favour: abiding. The apostolic benediction of grace — the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with you — displays the gracious close of the epistle: Paul invokes upon the believers the free favour and saving help of Christ, the sum of every blessing, the gentle benediction following close upon the solemn warning. The verse displays the desired grace of Christ, sealed by Paul's own love (v.24).
24:
"My love be with you all in Christ Jesus. Amen." The witnesses note Paul's closing assurance of his love to the whole church. On "my love be with you all," the grammatical voices note the affection. Barnes and Ellicott note Paul ends with a tender personal assurance: "my love be with you all" — his own affection embraces them, the whole church, despite all his reproofs and corrections; love is his last word.

On "you all," the witnesses note the breadth. Barnes notes the emphatic "all" — Paul's love extends to the entire church, including those he had rebuked, the factious and the erring; none is excluded from his affection.

On "in Christ Jesus. Amen," the grammatical-historical note the bond: his love is "in Christ Jesus" — a Christian love, grounded in their common union with Christ; and "Amen" seals the epistle. The verse displays Paul's closing assurance of his love to the whole church.

The witnesses agree: Paul ends with a tender assurance — "my love be with you all," his own affection embracing the whole church despite all his reproofs, love his last word (Barnes, Ellicott); the emphatic "all" extending his love even to those he had rebuked, none excluded (Barnes); his love "in Christ Jesus," grounded in their common union with him, and "Amen" sealing the epistle (grammatical-historical). The verse displays Paul's closing love. The verse marks the affection: my love. The verse displays the breadth: with you all. The verse displays the bond: in Christ Jesus. The verse displays the seal: Amen. The assurance displays Paul's love embracing the whole church. The verse displays the love: tender. The verse displays the all: none excluded. The verse displays the union: in Christ. Paul's closing assurance of his love to the whole church displays the affection that crowns the epistle: after all his reproofs and corrections, his last word is love — embracing them all, even the factious and erring, a Christian love grounded in their common union with Christ Jesus. The verse displays the tender close of the epistle, sealing the apostle's heart of love toward the church at Corinth.
